description: Synthetic 1500-word podcast transcript excerpt in the style of Infrastructure Investor / Catalyst-type interviews. NOT a real transcript. Hand-crafted to be plausible for measurement only.
user_query: Summarize this podcast segment using the six-section memo structure. Identify investment-relevant moments, name every firm, surface where the guest hedged.
source_content:
HOST: Welcome back. With me today is a managing partner at a mid-cap infrastructure GP focused on North American power and digital infrastructure. Let's start with the elephant in the room — PJM's reliability backstop procurement. The RFI window closes this week. What are you submitting?

GUEST: Thanks for having me. Look, I'll be careful what I say specifically because the bid window is still open, but at a thematic level, we've been positioned for this for about eighteen months. PJM's 14.9 GW UCAP target — that's roughly 20 GW nameplate when you reverse out the capacity factors — is the single biggest pull-through event for new gas in the East in a decade. The question isn't whether to participate. The question is which sites pencil at the cap factors PJM is willing to underwrite, and whether the supply chain can deliver Mitsubishi or GE turbines for COD by June 2031.

HOST: Mitsubishi's been quoting 36-month lead times. GE Vernova close behind. Where does that put greenfield economics?

GUEST: It puts a premium on optionality. The deals we're working hardest are the ones where you can either repower an existing site, take an in-development project from a developer who's blown through their committed equity, or — and this is the underrated path — acquire interconnection rights at a site that already has a queue position and gas lateral. The repower path is the cleanest because you inherit the air permit. The in-development carry-forward is messy but cheap. The queue-rights play is the highest IRR but the longest dated.

HOST: You haven't mentioned the obvious one — buying a regulated utility's GenCo carve-out. Blackstone-NiSource was the template. Are there more out there?

GUEST: There are three live ones I know of. I won't name them, but they're all in adjacent jurisdictions. The model works because the utility gets to monetize an asset they were going to retire anyway, the capacity stays in service for the IPP, and the regulator gets to claim they protected ratepayers. Blackstone got NIPSCO at — call it a multiple in the high single digits to low teens on EBITDA — but the real value capture was the ten-year tolling arrangement back to NIPSCO that locks in revenue. That's the structural innovation. It's not the asset purchase, it's the contract.

HOST: Let's talk about MISO. The DOE 202(c) orders kept Campbell, Coal Creek, others online past their planned retirement. NIPSCO has explicitly said they're scaling the GenCo to handle Amazon 2.4 GW plus Alphabet 515 MW. What does that tell you about where the next wave of carve-outs comes from?

GUEST: It tells you the regulator is now an active participant in deal sourcing, which is unprecedented. IURC has a PPA approval vote coming up — I think next week — that will set the template for how these tolling arrangements get blessed. If that vote goes the way the market expects, you'll see DTE, Ameren, and Evergy all explore similar structures within twelve months. The constraint is no longer regulatory will. The constraint is finding equity buyers willing to underwrite a 30-year asset on a 10-year contract.

HOST: Speaking of equity buyers — let's pivot to LP appetite. CP2 closed at $20.7 billion. That's a number that suggests outside equity is fully absorbed for US LNG project finance. Are you seeing the same?

GUEST: For Tier 1 sponsors with FERC-approved sites, yes, the equity window is functionally closed. NextDecade's T4 and T5 are the last bites at that table. T6 is in pre-filing — full FERC application expected mid-year — and service date is roughly 2030 for about 6 mtpa. The economics still work, but the LPs who chased CP2 are now over-allocated to the theme. Where I see the next wave is in the regas side, not the liquefaction side. Europe added eleven FSRUs since the war started. With the Hormuz situation and UAE's OPEC exit on May 1, the supply pull from Asia is going to compete with the European pull. That puts a bid under FSRU economics that wasn't there in 2024.

HOST: Who's the smart money in FSRU?

GUEST: Höegh, Excelerate are the obvious incumbents. The interesting question is who buys the next tranche of newbuilds. Stonepeak has been quietly active. ECP has a position. Brookfield's been looking. The problem is shipyard capacity — Hyundai, Samsung Heavy, Daewoo are booked through 2028 for FSRUs. So you're either buying existing units at a premium or you're buying shipyard slots, which is a different kind of trade.

HOST: Shifting to digital infrastructure — what do you make of the ComEd ICC docket on the data center queue?

GUEST: That's the canary. ComEd opened the investigation April 23. It's an eight-month process, so a decision around December. The driver is that they've got 75 customer applications totaling more than the system peak. That's not a queue, that's a backlog. The investigation is going to surface what every PUC is going to face within twelve months: who actually pays for the upsize? Today the marginal large-load customer pays, but the timeline mismatch — interconnection cost is incurred in year one, recovered over twenty — means somebody's eating cost-of-carry. The ICC's going to land on a sharing formula, and that formula will set the template for PJM, MISO, and ERCOT.

HOST: Brookfield and Compass officially walked away from a major Virginia DC project last month. What does that tell you?

GUEST: It tells you that interconnection certainty has become the binding constraint, not capital. Brookfield doesn't walk because of capital. They walk because the Dominion timeline went from 36 months to 84 months and the hyperscaler tenant wouldn't wait. Compass had committed equity but their tenant — I believe it was one of the big three clouds, but I can't confirm — bailed. The lesson is that the most valuable thing in DC development right now isn't land, isn't power, it's a signed interconnection agreement with a definitive ECD. Anybody holding one of those is sitting on something the market will pay a premium for.

HOST: Last topic — the contractor roll-up thesis. LGN, Faith Technologies, IES, Quanta. We've heard this thesis for years. What's different now?

GUEST: The math finally works. $255 billion addressable market, no player above 5% share. LGN's 2026 guidance is $3.7-3.9 billion revenue, $400-430 million EBITDA, backlog $3.7 billion up 49%. Data center is now 49% of their backlog. Faith Technologies is the number-one DC contractor for 2026 by ABC ranking, $1.6 billion revenue, Pittsboro Indiana expansion. The thesis is: pick three or four regional leaders, roll them up under a common platform, take the multiple from 8x EBITDA at the standalone level to 14-16x at the platform level. The risk is that you're paying for hyperscaler capex that may pull back in 2027 or 2028 if AI training demand softens.

HOST: Will it soften?

GUEST: I think the answer is yes, but later than people fear. Inference workloads are still ramping. Training has become more efficient on a per-token basis but the token volumes are still growing. So even if training capex flattens, inference capex picks up the slack through 2028 or 2029. After that, who knows. Capacity utilization on the existing fleet matters more than new builds at that point.

HOST: Thank you. We'll be right back.
